coredata.dc:title: A multi-dimensional anti-counterfeiting nanocomposite based on fluorescent CDs and Eu-MOFs with dual function for continuous detection of Cu2+ and Bacillus anthracis

Hu, Yousong
Xing, Luxin
Xu, Jun
Yue, Fengzhi
Zhang, Lina
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Traditional luminescent materials for anti-counterfeiting usually adopt encryption of low-level systems with limited security, which seriously hinders their application in preventing counterfeiting and information leakage. Therefore, it is urgent to develop materials for higher-level anti-counterfeiting. In this work, a stimulus-responsive intelligent luminescent material (AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs) was prepared by co-loading green-fluorescence CDs and red-fluorescence Eu-MOFs on Amino clay (AC). 5D security barcodes, which were easy to observe while difficult to clone, were ulteriorly designed based on the nanocomposite owing to the tunable fluorescence by optical stimulation and chemical stimulus. Owing to the large capacity, low cost, and easy authentication, the 5D security barcodes possess enormous potential in optical data storage and multi-dimensional information encryption. In addition, the chemical stimulus-response enables the nanocomposite achievable in detection of Cu2+ and Bacillus anthracis. Particularly, quantitative determination of copper in environmental water samples could be realized with a detection limit as low as 10.67 nM. Therefore, the material shows potential for detection of environmental pollutants besides advanced anti-counterfeiting.

head.ce:title: A multi-dimensional anti-counterfeiting nanocomposite based on fluorescent CDs and Eu-MOFs with dual function for continuous detection of Cu2+ and Bacillus anthracis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditional luminescent materials for anti-counterfeiting usually adopt encryption of low-level systems with limited security, which seriously hinders their application in preventing counterfeiting and information leakage. Therefore, it is urgent to develop materials for higher-level anti-counterfeiting. In this work, a stimulus-responsive intelligent luminescent material (AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs) was prepared by co-loading green-fluorescence CDs and red-fluorescence Eu-MOFs on Amino clay (AC). 5D security barcodes, which were easy to observe while difficult to clone, were ulteriorly designed based on the nanocomposite owing to the tunable fluorescence by optical stimulation and chemical stimulus. Owing to the large capacity, low cost, and easy authentication, the 5D security barcodes possess enormous potential in optical data storage and multi-dimensional information encryption. In addition, the chemical stimulus-response enables the nanocomposite achievable in detection of Cu2+ and Bacillus anthracis. Particularly, quantitative determination of copper in environmental water samples could be realized with a detection limit as low as 10.67 nM. Therefore, the material shows potential for detection of environmental pollutants besides advanced anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the trend of economic globalization and the accelerated process of modernization, the counterfeiting problem escalates accordingly and causes huge losses to the global economy, human health, and scientific development [1,2]. In recent years, the massive demand for anti-counterfeiting and information security has contributed to the rapid development of anti-counterfeiting technology, from the initial watermarking to laser hologram [3,4], magnetic response [5], radio frequency technology [6], nuclear track technology [7], photoluminescent materials [8–10], etc. Among them, luminescent anti-counterfeiting materials have received widespread attention, especially those with characteristic emission spectra in the visible range, which are accessible for visual reading with bright colors.

As to the luminescent materials for anti-counterfeiting, the encryption modes with single-level luminescence (one input and only one output) are limited in security and practical application in the protecting of confidential information, which are easily simulated and counterfeited [11–13]. Therefore, stimulus-responsive fluorescent materials standout as a representative of the latest generation of anti-counterfeiting technology with higher security, which can realize fluorescence switching or fluorescence color transmission under the stimuli of light [14]; 15a,16, temperature [17], pressure [18], pH [19], ions [20], organic small molecules [21], etc. Additionally, stimulus-responsive materials with luminescence show the advantages of fast response, high sensitivity and strong ability to display in real-time, which greatly broaden the application foreground in the area of advanced encryption and information storage [11,13]. However, the change in fluorescence signal is irreversible in some stimulus-responsive materials [22], inducing the encoded information in the data storage process to be irreversibly destroyed rather than temporarily hidden. Hence, it is urgent to develop new materials and manufacturing techniques to produce advanced stimulus-responsive luminescent materials to prevent sophisticated counterfeiting.

In recent years, photoluminescent materials based on organic dyes [15], metal-organic frameworks [16], carbon dots (CDs) [23], perovskite quantum dots [24] and rare earth nanoparticles [25] have been developed due to their bright multi-colored light emitted under light or other types of excitation and their high tunability. As porous crystal materials with ordered structure, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been paid much attention due to their attractive structure and huge potential in application, such as in gas storage, chemical separation, catalysis, luminescence anti-counterfeiting, etc. [26–29] Lanthanide-based MOFs are a special class of luminescent MOFs with unique fluorescence properties produced through f-f or f-d transitions in rare earth ions [30]. Besides, the luminescence intensity emitted by lanthanides is impressionable to the coordination environment, thus providing an efficient platform for stimulus-response [31]. These inherent luminescence properties of lanthanides and the unique advantages of MOFs provide Ln-MOFs with excellent prospects for multi-dimensional encryption of information.

Amino clay (AC) is a magnesium-containing organ-silicate clay with a special lamellar structure [32]. Benefitted from the electrostatic repulsion generated by a protonated amino group, amino clay owns good dispersibility and solubility in water and thus is ideal to be a soft carrier for Ln-MOFs and a perfect substrate for composite luminescent materials [33]. In the field of luminescence sensing, CDs stand out as a new luminescent material due to the advantages of low toxicity, low cost, strong photostability, and simple preparation process [34]. However, the means of anti-counterfeiting security based on the luminescence of single carbon dots are limited and easy to forge [35]. Therefore, carbon dots and Ln-MOFs could be coloaded in amino clay as the reference signal and response signal to construct a sensing platform for multi-dimensional information encryption.

According to the current situation, a significant quantity of reversible stimulus-responsive luminescent materials have been developed. At the same time, there has been little research on the combination of amino clay (AC), carbon dots, and lanthanide MOFs for anti-counterfeiting. Herein, a stimulus-responsive intelligent luminescent material based on amino clay (AC) was designed and assembled as shown in Scheme 1 . Specifically, double-emission fluorescent nano-sensor (AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs) was prepared with CDs being encapsulated in amino clay as a reference signal and Eu-MOFs being grafted on the surface as a recognition unit. The nano-sensor emitted red fluorescence under the 254 nm UV lamp, where the red fluorescence of Eu-MOFs surpassed the green fluorescence emitted by CDs. After the Cu2+-containing solution was added, the fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs gradually decreased. In contrast, the characteristic fluorescence of CDs was basically unaffected, and thus caused a transitional change of the fluorescence from red to green in the system. By adding DPA, the fluorescence of Eu-MOFs was subsequently recovered and gradually exceeded the green fluorescence of CDs, achieving an "on-off-on" process. The composite material was further made into different luminous inks, and 5D anti-counterfeiting barcodes were produced by luminous printing according to the multi-mode emission and stimulus-response of CDs and Eu-MOFs. The material's unique stimulus-response behavior and multi-channel fluorescence signal provide a reference for constructing new environmentally friendly double-emission fluorescence materials for multi-dimensional encryption and decryption of information.

Magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O) and 3-amino-propyl triethoxy silane (APTES, 99.00 %) were purchased separately from Shanghai Titan Chemical Co., LTD. and Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. Fluorescein was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., LTD. Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, 99.99 %), 2,6-pyridine dicarboxylic acid (DPA, 99.00 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96.00 %) were all purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., LTD. In the experiment, the water used was deionized, and the reagents were all analytical-grade reagents without any purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDX) were carried out by Tecnai-G2-F30 at an accelerated voltage of 300 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was determined by Thermo Scientific Esca Lab 250Xi. Spectral analysis was performed on an ultraviolet spectrophotometer, which is PerkinElmer Lambda 950 type. P-XRD patterns in the range of 5–80° were obtained with Cu-kα radiation on a Rigaku-Dmax2400 X-ray diffractometer. The infrared spectra in the wave number range of 4000-400 cm−1 were measured based on KBr powder by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 Fourier infrared spectrometer with a resolution of 4 wave numbers and 32 scans. The measurement for the content of rare earth elements was carried out on Agilent ICP-OES 725 ES. The Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer was applied to obtain the fluorescence spectra and the steady-state luminescence spectra of the sample in an aqueous solution at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots (CDs) with green fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: 200 mg of fluorescein was added to 30 mL of 0.67 M NaOH solution and received ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to be completely dissolved. The mixture was then placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and pyrolyzed at 200 °C for 8 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the pyrolysis products underwent dialysis with 1000 DA for three days. Then, the solids obtained after freeze-drying were sealed in a dry environment.

The Eu-MOFs were synthesized according to the reference method [36] with minor modifications. 111.5 mg of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.250 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol with ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, and then 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol with 62.5 mg of DPA (0.374 mmol) was added in the former ethanol solution as stirring. After stirring at room temperature for 1 h, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 72 h. The unreacted precursors were removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed alternately with ethanol and deionized water three times. The final products were collected by heating in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 12 h.

Firstly, 2 mg of carbon dots were dispersed with ultrasonic treatment in 10 mL of ethanol, and then 50 mg of Eu-MOFs in 15 mL ethanol solution were added after stirring magnetically for 10 min. Secondly, 168 mg of MgCl2·6H2O (0.826 mmol) was dissolved in 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol and then added into the former mixture under agitation. Thirdly, 0.246 g of APTES (1.11 mmol) was added after the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Finally, the precipitates were collected centrifugally and washed with ethanol three times to remove the incomplete reactants. The end product was obtained after heat treatment in a vacuum at 60 °C for 1 day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and application of fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilic fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink based on rare earth fluorescent materials exhibits huge market value as the trend of green printing today. The preparation method of the hydrophilic anti-counterfeiting ink was as follows. Firstly, the optimal viscosity solution was prepared with ethanol/water (v/v = 10:20), then 20 mL glycerin was added drop by drop. Later on, the mixture was stirred for 1 h and treated by ultrasonic for 1 h. Finally, the hydrophilic anti-counterfeiting ink was obtained by dispersing the multi-color anti-counterfeiting material in 5 mL of the above solution by ultrasonic for 20 min. The inks based on CDs with green fluorescence and Eu-MOFs with red fluorescence were synthesized in the same way as above. Filter paper with almost no background luminescence was used to test of information erasure and coding. The encrypted information was decrypted and re-encrypted by spraying copper ions and DPA solution successively onto the filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the structure and morphology of nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs, Eu-MOFs, and AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs were characterized by TEM to observe the microstructure changes of the materials during preparation. The prepared CDs showed good mono-dispersity and uniform distribution with a diameter of about 1.3 nm (Fig. 1 a). Eu-MOFs were observed as uniformly distributed spheres with a diameter of about 200–500 nm (Fig. 1b). The transmission electron microscope image of nanocomposite AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs represented that there were uniform distribution of CDs and Eu-MOFs on the surface of lamellar structure in the amino clay (Fig. 1c). These morphological characteristics endowed the nanocomposites with good stability and dispersion in solution to form transparent luminous ink.

To better explore the microstructure and element composition, the chemical element composition of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs was studied by energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) besides TEM. There were N and Eu elements in the nanocomposites in addition to C, O, Mg, Cl, Cu, and Si (Fig. 1d), suggesting that AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs were successfully formed. Furthermore, the content of Eu3+ in the synthesized nanocomposites was measured 6.4 percent by ICP-AES, indicating that Eu-MOFs were successfully incorporated onto the surface of the layered nanocomposites.

The analysis of the XRD diffraction pattern further proved the formation of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. S1a. There was a relatively broad diffraction peak near 2θ = 21.9° in CDs due to massive sp3 defects and the amorphous property caused by the disordered ordering of carbon atoms. Moreover, Eu-MOFs were demonstrated to have been successfully prepared by the position of the diffraction peak of Eu-MOFs by that reported by Wang et al. [36] There was an inevitable overlap of a diffraction peak at 21.6° between the amino clay and CDs. Meanwhile, the related diffraction peaks of Eu-MOFs remained well on AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs, indicating the nanocomposite was well prepared. The stability of Eu-MOF can be further determined by PXRD measurements. The framework exhibits good stability in acidic and basic aqueous solutions (pH = 3–12) as shown in Fig. S2a. However, the framework is stable before 180 °C. When the temperature is up to 220 °C, some peaks of PXRD disappear (Fig. S2b), which indicated that the high temperature destroys the crystal structure.

The chemical bonds and functional groups of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs were studied clearly and comprehensively according to Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. S1b, AC and AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs revealed similar absorption bands at 3717, 2931, 1021, and 1009 cm−1, corresponding to stretching vibrations of –OH, –CH2, Si–O–Si, and Si–O, respectively. The absorption peaks of CDs at 1616, 1578, and 1508 cm−1 were mainly due to the skeleton vibration of the aromatic ring, indicating the aromatic structure in CDs [37,38]. In the meantime, the characteristic peaks of CDs at 3088 and 1616 cm−1 were matched well with stretching vibrations of –CC–H [39].

To clarify the elemental composition and surface states of the composites, AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 2 a). Obvious diffraction peaks at 1304.63, 531.97, 399.81, 285.12 and 102.33 eV were found in the full spectrum of XPS for AC, attributing to Mg 1s, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and Si 2p, respectively. Compared with that of pure AC, the XPS spectrum of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs exhibited additional peaks at 1134.75 eV and 1164.85 eV ascribed to Eu 3d, indicating that Eu-MOFs were encapsulated in the amino clay matrix. In the meantime, the significantly enhanced intensity of C 1s in the spectrum of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs suggested the presence of CDs and Eu-MOFs on the surface or between the layers of the nanocomposites.

In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s shown in Fig. 2b, there were C–C/C–H/CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.1 eV), C–O (285.8 eV) [40] and COOH (288.8 eV), indicating that the element C existed in the composites with hybrid type of sp2 and sp3. The hybrid type of element C once again demonstrated the formation of carbon dots [41]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s included three peaks (Fig. 2c) respectively put down to C–N (399.6 eV), N–H (401.6 eV), and N–O (407.1 eV) bonds [42,43], which confirms the successful loading of Eu-MOFs onto the clay matrix. As for the high-resolution map of Eu 3d (Fig. 2d), four peaks were observed resulted from multiple splits of the spin orbits, where the former peaks at 1134.6 eV and 1124.4 eV belong to Eu3+ 3d5/2 and the latter peaks belong to Eu3+ 3d3/2, respectively [40].

Therefore, The FT-IR and XPS spectroscopy studies indicated comprehensively the existence of functional groups –COOH, –NH2, and –OH on the surface of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs, whose dispersion in water was improved by these hydrophilic groups. Hence, according to previous reports, the composites were easily prepared as transparent luminous ink.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on sensing mechanism of the fluorescent nano-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CDs exhibited strong green fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp (254 nm). As is shown in Fig. 3 a, when the excitation wavelength continuously changed from 280 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence spectra of CDs constantly kept the maximum fluorescence at 613 nm with the shifty fluorescence intensity, implying that the excitation wavelength barely affected the emission position of CDs but the fluorescence intensity. When the maximum fluorescence wavelength of 613 nm was fixed, the excitation spectra of CDs presented the best excitation at 290 nm and 325 nm. In addition, the response of CDs to copper ions was studied, which proved that the fluorescence strength of CDs remained unchanged with the incremental concentration of copper ions (Fig. S3). Therefore, the green-fluorescent CDs were quite suitable as the reference signal to the stimulus-response of copper ions.

The Ultraviolet–Visible absorption spectra, excitation spectra, and emission spectra of Eu-MOFs were collected and shown in Fig. 3b. Under 280 nm excitation, Eu-MOFs emitted red fluorescence with the maximum emission at 616 nm responding to the 5D0→7F2 electron transition of Eu3+. When the fluorescence wavelength was fixed at 616 nm, there were two absorption peaks at 271 and 279 nm showed in the excitation spectra, ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic CC bond and pyridine ring, respectively [44,45]. As for the excitation wavelength of the composites, a trade-off must be made to take the luminescence characteristics of CDs and Eu-MOFs into account. Therefore, ultraviolet light of 280 nm was selected as excitation in the subsequent fluorescence studies. Under the excitation of 280 nm, the characteristic peaks of both CDs and Eu-MOFs were observed in the fluorescence spectra of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs (Fig. 3c), including three emission peaks at 513 nm, 595 nm, and 616 nm, respectively. The above fluorescence studies manifested that the fluorescence properties of CDs and Eu-MOFs were well retained after embedding into the amino clay matrix, barely affected by the preparation process. The process was verified by the fluorescence spectra of CDs, Eu-MOFs, AC, AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs, and the supernatant of the composites (Fig. 3d). The results indicated that CDs and Eu-MOFs in the material were coated in AC but not attached to the surface due to mechanical mixing.

The stimulus-response of the nano-sensor to Cu2+ and DPA was evaluated systematically by fluorescence titration (Section S1). The luminescence spectra of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs nanomaterial with or without the addition of Cu2+ and DPA, when excited by ultraviolet light of 280 nm, were revealed in Fig. 4 a. The composites showed main fluorescence emission at 513 nm and stronger emission at 616 nm without the presence of copper ions. Hence, the composite emerged with red fluorescence under 280 nm excitation and benefitted from the higher fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs. However, the emitted fluorescence of the nano-sensor changed from red to green as 10 μM of Cu2+-containing solution was added. The fluorescence spectra revealed that the emission intensity of CDs was not affected by adding Cu2+. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm rapidly declined because Cu2+ was a better coordination center with DPA [46]. Therefore, the fluorescence of Eu-MOFs profited from the antenna effect by DPA was greatly weakened to be lower than that of CDs, resulting in the changed fluorescence color.

Then, when stimulated with DPA, the luminescence of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs at 616 nm was turned on again, which was attributed to the fact that DPA could displace the water molecules coordinated to Eu3+ and efficiently transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+ (illustration on the left of Fig. 4a) [47], which was known as "antenna effect". In the process of two-step stimulation, the significant change of fluorescence color from red to green to red in AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs solution was observed under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp (illustration on the right of Fig. 4a), and the "ON-OFF-ON" process of fluorescence was realized (Fig. 4b). The superior optical characteristics in this process effectively improved the optical encoding capacity and security, providing a new perspective for the application of rare-earth-based functional materials in the field of optical coding materials.

The fluorescence lifetime attenuation curve of Eu3+ was measured at 280 nm to elucidate the response mechanism of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs (Fig. 4c). The changes in fluorescence lifetime for Eu3+ (at 280 nm) in the composites were analyzed in OriginPro 2021 with the double exponential function, and the corresponding formulas (1) and (2) were applied to calculate the average decay lifetime of Eu3+ before and after the continuous addition of Cu2+ and DPA to the composite, as shown in Table S1. The average value (τav) was calculated to be 0.490 ms when fitting the decay trajectory of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs with the corresponding formulas (1) and (2). When copper ions were added to the composite, the average value (τav) decreased to 0.354 ms and grew back to 0.406 ms with the addition of DPA, which was consistent with the fluorescence mechanism discussed above.

Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra provided specific evidence for this phenomenon ulteriorly. There were two obvious UV absorption peaks at 271.3 nm and 279.1 nm for AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs, and both peaks exhibited varying degrees of blue shift and reduced fluorescence intensity after Cu2+ ions were added. Among them, the former shifted from 271.3 nm to 268.8 nm. The results were that DPA, originally coordinated with Eu3+ in the composites, was snatched away by additive Cu2+ and formed a new complex with it. When DPA was subsequently added to the above mixture, the UV absorption peaks almost restored to consistency with those of the original composites, indicating that the free DPA with greater affinity could coordinate with Cu2+ to release originally coordinated DPA to recover the fluorescence of Eu3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions in stimulus-response process
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the outstanding fluorescence characteristics, AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs show excellent potential in visual detection of trace copper ions. Before conducting detection for copper ions, the experimental conditions of reaction time, temperature, and pH value were tested to ensure the best detection performance of the ratio fluorescence probe. As shown in Fig. S4a, the intensity ratio I616/I515 of the nano-sensor AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs was tested within 0–10 min after 10 μM of Cu2+ was added, suggesting that the response of the nano-sensor for copper ions leveled off after 1 min. Therefore, the best incubation time was ascribed to 1 min in the subsequent experiments. Since the working temperature in the laboratory is in the range of 5–40 °C, the influence of temperature on the sensor performance was explored when 10 μM of Cu2+ was added, and the result demonstrated that temperature exhibited little effect on the detection performance (Fig. S4b). With the additive of Cu2+, the intensity ratio I616/I515 of the sensor maintained stable in the range of pH = 3.0–11.0 (Fig. S4c), indicating that the sensor owned a certain resistance to acid and alkali. Besides, a significant change was hardly observed in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the sensor (I616/I515) within 30 days (Fig. S4d), manifesting that the composite possessed good fluorescence stability at room temperature. In summary, the fast response time and excellent stability of pH, temperature and thermodynamic stability jointly determined AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs as a promising platform for fluorescence biosensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of fluorescent encryption-decryption-Re-encryption in information transmission
ce:section.ce:para: Benefitting greatly from the sensitive response to Cu2+ and DPA, AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs would be applied for continuous detection of water pollutants and further designed for semi-quantitative analysis visually integrated with a smartphone (Section S2). What is even more remarkable, the intelligent sensor AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs was applied in the field of information security by combining with binary coding rules based on the excellent fluorescence characteristics induced by stimulus-response (Fig. 5 a). In the encryption process, the red commercial luminous ink with stable fluorescence intensity was defined as "0″, and the green luminous ink (AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs) stimulated by Cu2+ was defined as "1". The letters "CDS" were expressed by binary codes 01000011, 01000100, and 01010011 and written on filter paper with a gold pen in the two kinds of luminous ink. The written binary codes were invisible with irradiation in visible light while hidden in the invalid message by being read as three invalid messages "00000000″ under a UV lamp of 254 nm. During the decryption process, the fluorescence color of the prepared luminous ink based on AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs changed from red to green to decrypt the hidden information by spraying a solution containing Cu2+, and the corresponding information "CDS" was found according to the standard ASCII code comparison table. Since the external stimulus of DPA could restore the fluorescence of the Eu-emitting, the hidden information could be re-encrypted into an invalid state by spraying a solution containing DPA, thus facilitating information transmission.

In addition, the word "CARDS" was written on non-fluorescent filter paper, where the letters to be protected (C, D, and S) were written in prepared ink with green developing. In contrast, the remaining letters were written in commercial red ink. When the filter paper was put under an ultraviolet lamp of 254 nm, all the letters were observed to emit red fluorescence (Fig. 5b), indicating that the information to be protected (C, D, and S) was well hidden to convey wrong information. Similarly, when Cu2+-solution was sprayed onto the filter paper, the letters to be protected (C, D, and S) were decrypted to show green fluorescence, in sharp contrast to the surrounding information (A and R), still emitting red fluorescence. Then, the green fluorescence information of "CDS" could be re-encrypted by spraying DPA-solution to prevent information leakage. Therefore, the above results demonstrated that AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs as intelligent sensors possessed high security in information storage and transmission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-mode fluorescence characteristics and anti-counterfeiting performance
ce:section.ce:para: Since carbon dots of green fluorescence and Eu-MOFs of red fluorescence are excited by specific excitation wavelengths, the prepared composite nano-sensor emits three different fluorescence colors of red, yellow, and green at 254 nm, 302 nm, and 365 nm, respectively. (Fig. 6 a). Agarose is a polysaccharide derived from Marine red algae with polygalactose as the main component. Agarose hydrogel has been widely used in cosmetics, drug delivery, and anti-counterfeiting fields owing to its non-toxic, excellent mechanical properties, high biological activity, high stability, and biodegradability [48]. In this work, a series of fluorescence anti-counterfeiting models of agarose hydrogels with different shapes were prepared by combing the three-mode fluorescence property of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs with agarose hydrogels free of fluorescent interference in the area of anti-counterfeiting. These hydrogels presented light yellow and hardly glowed under sunlight (Fig. 6b) while emitting bright red fluorescence under the irradiation of a 254 nm lamp, green fluorescence at 302 nm, and green fluorescence at 365 nm, respectively. Hence, the AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs agarose hydrogel pattern showed a prominent prospect in anti-counterfeiting applications based on the multi-channel luminescence form.

Many luminescent anti-counterfeiting materials are usually reported as one-dimensional encryption systems with one input and one output. Such materials are liable to be forged with limited security and low anti-counterfeiting levels, severely restricting the application in the transmission of confidential information [49,50]. To improve the security level, 5D anti-counterfeiting barcodes were designed by combining the stimulus-response ability and three-mode luminescence characteristics of the intelligent sensor AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs.

To achieve 5D security, three hydrophilic luminescent inks were prepared based on Eu-MOFs, CDs, and AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs, respectively. The 5D barcodes were defined based on the standard Code-93 rule as shown in Fig. 7 a. The dimensional codes were composed of bars and spaces with different widths [51]. The first-dimensional code (1stD code) was defined as "1″, "2″, and "3″ according to the width, and the other four-dimensional verified codes were defined based on the fluorescence colors. Therefore, the three-dimensional verified codes (2ndD code, 3rdD code, and 4thD code) were created based on different fluorescence colors under excitation of different wavelengths. Specifically, the red and green fluorescence was marked separately as "1″ and "2″ in 2ndD code when stimulated by UV excitation of 254 nm; the red, green, and yellow fluorescence was labeled "1″, "2″, and "3″ respectively under UV lamp of 302 nm in 3rdD code. In comparison, the bright green fluorescence was labeled "1″ under irradiation of 365 nm in the 4thD code. The 5thD code was established based on the different responses of the three inks to stimulation of copper ion, where "1″ and "2″ represented red and green fluorescence, respectively. Both the bars failed to emit fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp, and spaces without fluorescent ink were labeled "0″ in the four-dimensional codes.

In the coding process, the dimensional codes other than 1stD code were designed by printing barcodes the same width as 1stD code while filled with three hydrophilic luminescent inks prepared based on Eu-MOFs, CDs, and AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs in the bars (Fig. 7b). In the decryption process, the 1stD code was read out as [1 1 3 2 2 1] according to the width of the bars and spaces as described earlier, and the arrangement of the fluorescent bar and the non-fluorescent space decrypted the other four-dimensional codes. For instance, the 2ndD code was interpreted by the fluorescence emission color under the exposure of the 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. The information of the two-dimensional codes can be collected as113221 101020. Similarly, two additional barcodes containing information for the 1stD code and additional 3rdD were read out under 302 nm as 113221 103020, and the two-dimensional codes of the 1stD code and additional 4thD codes were decrypted as 113221 001010under 365 nm UV lamp.

For the 5thD code, the conception was established according to the fluorescence mechanism described above (Fig. 7b and c). As copper ion has a stronger coordination affinity for DPA than Eu3+, the fluorescence of Eu-emitting in Eu-MOFs would be reduced by Cu2+. In contrast, the green fluorescence of CDs would not be affected. However, a small amount of Cu2+ solution failed to trigger the variation of fluorescent color in Eu-MOFs that was visible to the naked eye. However, Eu-MOFs in the composite (Ink 3) are more sensitive to copper ions due to the dispersion and fixation of the amino clay matrix. Additionally, the dynamic change of fluorescence color in the composite (Ink 3) was more evident with the green CDs as the reference signal. Therefore, when stimulated by Cu2+ solution, ink 1 and ink 2 kept the red and green fluorescence unchanged, respectively, while the fluorescence color of Ink 3 changed from red to green under irradiation of 254 nm. Then, two additional barcodes of the 1stD code and additional 5thD were interpreted as113221 102020 under a UV lamp of 254 nm. Additionally, the 5thD code could also be encrypted by spraying DPA solution, which is misinterpreted as [102010]. Combined with these four results, the 5D illuminated barcode could be decrypted to
[

.

Unlike the limited one-dimensional code in the traditional Code-93 system (56 possible combinations of bars and spaces), the 5D luminescent security barcode could generate a much larger combination of codes to improve the information storage capacity and security level greatly. When the 1stD code in the 5D luminescent barcodes was fixed unchanged, coding combinations of the other four-dimensional codes were calculated to be 24 possibilities (4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 24). Therefore, totaling 1344 possible combinations (N = 24 × 56) would be generated with various widths and spaces in different FL-emission colors. The 5D fluorescent barcodes are more suitable for multiple encryption strategies with excellent characteristics of easy authentication than the anti-counterfeiting methods in the literature [52–54]. Hence, the prepared 5D barcodes can realize fast coding and anti-counterfeiting encryption of multi-level authentication information and meanwhile maintain a favorable appearance which is convenient to check.

In this work, a reversible stimulus-responsive nano-sensor was designed and prepared based on the characteristic fluorescence of both CDs and Eu (III)-MOFs loaded concurrently on amino clay. AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs was highly sensitive to Cu2+ stimulation with a detection limit as low as 10.67 nM measured by the ratio fluorescence method, which could be applied to the quantitative determination of copper in environmental water samples. Real-time detection of Cu2+ could be achieved on-site through color capture and smartphone analysis applications. Furthermore, the intelligent sensor could be applied in the preparation of anti-counterfeiting fluorescent inks and 5D anti-counterfeiting barcodes for optical data storage based on the three-mode luminescence and stimulus-responsive fluorescence properties of AC@CDs-Eu-MOFs. Only by spraying a Cu2+ solution, can the encrypted content of the stimulus-response ink be deciphered under ultraviolet light, which would be encrypted again after the stimulation of the specific ligand DPA. The prepared 5D security barcodes were high capacity, easy to authenticate, and difficult to imitate with increased security, and the excellent linear color coordination and chemical stimulus response endow them with practical applications prospects in barcode systems.
